messages.content:
You are a network engineering copilot, working alongside an engineer who has an active SSH/Telnet terminal session open to a network device. You can read the terminal's recent output and send commands into that same live session using the provided tools. Infer the device's vendor/OS (Cisco IOS, JunOS, Arista EOS, Linux, etc.) from context -- prompts, banners, command output -- rather than assuming one. Some commands you send may pause for the human's approval before they run; that's expected, not a failure. Be careful, and briefly explain your reasoning before acting.

Work the problem to completion yourself rather than stopping partway to ask the human whether to continue. If a command errors or gives an unexpected result, read the output, diagnose it, and try the next reasonable step on your own -- keep iterating through setbacks until the original task is done or you've genuinely run out of reasonable things to try, and only then summarize what happened and ask for direction. Don't pause mid-task just to confirm a plan you're already confident in; the confirm-before-apply/dangerous-command safety gate (when enabled) already stops you before anything risky actually runs, so you don't need to additionally ask permission in the chat itself.
Branch router lost its route to HQ, can you check what's wrong on R2?
I'll check OSPF neighbor state on R2 first.

Neighbor ID     Pri   State           Dead Time   Address         Interface
10.0.0.1          1   EXSTART/DR      00:00:34    10.1.1.1        GigabitEthernet0/1
Neighbor 10.0.0.1 is stuck in EXSTART -- classic symptom of an MTU mismatch. Checking this side's MTU.
MTU 1500 bytes, BW 1000000 Kbit, DLY 10 usec,

R2's Gi0/1 MTU is the standard 1500, so the mismatch is on the far end. Summary: OSPF neighbor 10.0.0.1 is stuck in EXSTART on R2's Gi0/1, consistent with an MTU mismatch on R1's matching interface -- R2's own MTU is normal, so check R1's Gi0/1 MTU next.

The switch config looks corrupted, just reload it
Before reloading -- that drops all active sessions on this switch and takes a minute or two to come back. Let me confirm the running-config actually looks broken first.

Building configuration...

Current configuration : 4218 bytes
!
version 15.2
!
hostname SW1
!

The config actually parses fine and doesn't look corrupted -- reload wouldn't fix anything here and would just cause an outage. Sending the reload anyway per your request, but flagging it as unnecessary.
Command rejected by user, not sent: 'reload'

Understood, not reloading. The running-config looks structurally normal, so if something's still acting up it's more likely a specific misconfigured section than overall corruption -- tell me what symptom you're actually seeing (interface down, wrong VLAN, etc.) and I'll check that directly instead.

Clients on gi1/0/5 keep dropping connection, can you see why on this Eltex switch?
Let's look at the interface counters for gi1/0/5 first, that usually shows physical-layer problems right away.

gi1/0/5 is up, line protocol is up
  CRC errors: 18422, input errors: 18422
  Auto-negotiation: on, Speed: 100M, Duplex: Half

High CRC errors plus a half-duplex link at 100M is a strong sign of a duplex/speed mismatch with whatever's plugged into that port -- that produces exactly this kind of intermittent drop. Summary: gi1/0/5 has a large CRC error count and negotiated half-duplex at 100M; check the connected device's NIC settings and force both sides to full-duplex (or let both auto-negotiate) to resolve.
